en.extract:
The loggerhead kingbird (Tyrannus caudifasciatus) is a species of sub-oscine passerine bird belonging to the family Tyrannidae, the tyrant flycatchers. This species is found in wooded habitats in the islands of the northern Caribbean, with records of vagrants from Florida.


Taxonomy
The loggerhead kingbird was first formally described in 1839 by the French naturalist Alcide d'Orbigny with its type locality given as Cuba. This species is classified within the genus Tyrannus within the family Tyrannidae, the tyrant or New World flycatchers. The Tyrannidae are placed within the parvorder Tyrannides within the order Passeriformes, the perching birds.


Subspecies
The loggerhead kingbird is subdivided into the following subspecies:

T. c. caudifasciatus D'Orbigny, 1839 - Cuba
T. c. flavescens  Parkes, 1963 - Isle of Pines
T. c. bahamensis Bryant, 1864 - Grand Bahama, Abaco, Andros and New Providence islands
T. c. caymanensis Nicoll, 1904 - Cayman Islands
T. c. jamaicensis Chapman, 1892 - Jamaica
T. c. taylori Sclater, 1864 - Puerto Rico and Vieques I.
T. c. gabbii  Lawrence, 1876 - Hispaniola
The Puerto Rican subspecies, T. c. taylori, has been proposed as a separate species, the Puerto Rican kingbird (T. taylori).


Description
The loggerhead kingbird has a length of 24 to 26 cm (9.4 to 10.2 in), and is a large species of kingbird with a heavy bill. It is two-toned dark upper parts and lighter underparts. The head, apart from the throat, is black with an orange patch on the topmost part of the crown which is rarely seen. The upperparts are gray and the underparts are lighter in color. In most populations there is a grayish band at the end of the tail, although this can be white.


Distribution and habitat
The loggerhead kingbird is found in throughout the northern West Indies, in the Bahamas, Cayman Islands, Cuba, Isla de la Juventud, Hispaniola (the Dominican Republic and Haiti), Jamaica and Puerto Rico. It very rarely occurs in southern Florida as a vagrant. Its natural habitats are woodlands, pine and broad-leafed forest, shade coffee palntations, mangroves and open park-like habitats. It can be found from the coast to into the lower parts of mountainous areas.


Biology
The loggerhead kingbird feeds on flying insects, small fruit and berries, and small lizards. It is frequently observed hunting from exposed perches. In Puerto Rico this species builds a cup shaped nest, fabricated from twigs, stems and grass. The pair vocalize constantly during courtship, making rattling and other loud calls, persistently chasing one another. Both male and female choose the nest site and once the selection has been made then the pair make low glides over the site before the female perches on the site. When she perches she flattens herself against the supporting branch, turning in a circle while vocalising and fluttering her wings while the males joins in the vocalizing and erects his normally concealed.  brightly colored crown feathers. On Puerto Rico breeding is mainly observed between February and July but nest building can begin in  November. The clutch size is 2–4 eggs, except Jamaica where 4–5 eggs was the average. The adults are very aggressive towards other birds invading their territories and this may be why shiny cowbird (molothurus bonairensis) nest parasitism was not recorded for this species. It is a sedentary species, unlike the related gray kingbird (T. dominicensis) and it will move into the preferred habitat of the gray kingbird when the latter migrates away from the islands in winter.


Gallery


References

The '''loggerhead kingbird''' ('''''Tyrannus caudifasciatus''''') is a [[species]] of [[Tyranni|sub-oscine]] [[passerine]] bird belonging to the [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Tyrannidae]], the [[tyrant flycatcher]]s. This species is found in wooded habitats in the islands of the northern Caribbean, with records of [[Vagrancy (biology)|vagrants]] from [[Florida]].<ref name="northamerica">{{cite book |isbn=9780691117966 |title=Rare Birds of North America |first1=Steve |last1 = Howell |first2=Ian |last2=Lewington |first3=Will |last3=Russel |page=356 |publisher=Princeton University Press |year=2014}}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
The loggerhead kingbird was first formally [[Species description|described]] in 1839 by the French [[naturalist]] [[Alcide d'Orbigny]] with its [[Type locality (biology)|type locality]] given as Cuba.<ref name = d'Orbigny>{{cite book |author=Alcide d'Orbigny |author-link=Alcide d'Orbigny |year=1839 |series=Historia fisica, politica y natural de la isla de Cuba |language=Es |title=Mamiferos y Aves |chapter=Aves |page=70 |publisher= |location=Paris |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/5977#page/42/mode/1up}}</ref> This species is classified within the [[genus]] ''[[Tyrannus]]'' within the family Tyrannidae, the tyrant or New World flycatchers. The Tyrannidae are placed within the [[parvorder]] [[Tyrannides]] within the [[Order (biology)|order]] [[Passeriformes]], the perching birds.<ref name = Ohlson>{{cite journal |author=Jan N I. Ohlson |author2=Martin Irestedt |author3=Per G. P. Ericson |author4=Jon Fieldsa |year=2013 |title=Phylogeny and classification of the New World suboscines (Aves, Passeriformes) |journal=Zootaxa |volume=3613 |issue=1 |pages=1–35 |doi= 10.11646/zootaxa.3613.1.1|pmid=24698900 }}</ref>

===Subspecies===
The loggerhead kingbird is subdivided into the following [[subspecies]]:<ref name = Avibase>{{cite web |url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?lang=EN&avibaseid=861588BF&sec=summary&ssver=1 |title=Loggerhead Kingbird ''Tyrannus caudifasciatus'' d'Orbigny, ACVMD 1839 | access-date=16 June 2024 |website=Avibase}}</ref>
* ''T. c. caudifasciatus'' <small>D'Orbigny, 1839</small> - Cuba
* ''T. c. flavescens''  <small>[[Kenneth Carroll Parkes|Parkes]], 1963</small> - [[Isla de la Juventud|Isle of Pines]]
* ''T. c. bahamensis'' <small>[[Henry Bryant (naturalist)|Bryant]], 1864</small> - [[Grand Bahama]], [[Abaco Islands|Abaco]], [[Andros, Bahamas|Andros]] and [[New Providence]] islands
* ''T. c. caymanensis'' <small>[[Michael John Nicoll|Nicoll]], 1904</small> - [[Cayman Islands]]
* ''T. c. jamaicensis'' <small>[[Frank Chapman (ornithologist)|Chapman]], 1892</small> - Jamaica
* ''T. c. taylori'' <small>[[Philip Sclater|Sclater]], 1864</small> - Puerto Rico and [[Vieques, Puerto Rico|Vieques I.]]
* ''T. c. gabbii''  <small>[[George Newbold Lawrence|Lawrence]], 1876</small> - [[Hispaniola]]

The Puerto Rican subspecies, ''T. c. taylori'', has been proposed as a separate species, the Puerto Rican kingbird (''T. taylori'').<ref name = BPR/>

==Description==
The loggerhead kingbird has a length of {{cvt|24|to|26|cm}}, and is a large species of kingbird with a heavy bill. It is two-toned dark upper parts and lighter underparts. The head, apart from the throat, is black with an orange patch on the topmost part of the crown which is rarely seen.<ref name = BWI>{{cite book |author=Herbert Raffaele |author2=James Wiley |author3=Orlando Garrido |author4=Allan Keith |author5=Janis Raffaele |year=2003 |title=Birds of the West Indies |series=Helm Field Guides |publisher=Christopher Helm |isbn=978-0-7136-5419-6 |page=120}}</ref> The upperparts are gray and the underparts are lighter in color. In most populations there is a grayish band at the end of the tail, although this can be white.<ref name = eBird>{{cite web |url=https://ebird.org/species/logkin |title=Loggerhead Kingbird ''Tyrannus caudifasciatus'' |access-date=16 June 2024 |website=[[eBird]] |publisher=[[Cornell Lab of Ornithology]]}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
The loggerhead kingbird is found in throughout the northern [[West Indies]], in the [[Bahamas]], [[Cayman Islands]], [[Cuba]], [[Isla de la Juventud]], [[Hispaniola]] (the [[Dominican Republic]] and [[Haiti]]), [[Jamaica]] and [[Puerto Rico]].<ref name = Avibase/> It very rarely occurs in southern [[Florida]] as a vagrant.<ref name = BNA>{{cite web |url=https://www.birds-of-north-america.net/Loggerhead_Kingbird.html#:~:text=The%20Loggerhead%20Kingbird%20is%20a,but%20displays%20a%20bulkier%20body. |title=Loggerhead Kingbird |access-date=16 June 2024 |website=Bords of North America |publisher=Brian Young}}</ref><ref name = eBird/> Its natural [[habitat]]s are woodlands, pine and broad-leafed forest, shade coffee palntations, mangroves and open park-like habitats. It can be found from the coast to into the lower parts of mountainous areas.<ref name = BWI/>

==Biology==
The loggerhead kingbird feeds on flying insects, small fruit and berries, and small lizards.<ref name=BofCuba/> It is frequently observed hunting from exposed perches.<ref name = BWI/> In Puerto Rico this species builds a cup shaped nest, fabricated from twigs, stems and grass. The pair vocalize constantly during courtship, making rattling and other loud calls, persistently chasing one another. Both male and female choose the nest site and once the selection has been made then the pair make low glides over the site before the female perches on the site. When she perches she flattens herself against the supporting branch, turning in a circle while vocalising and fluttering her wings while the males joins in the vocalizing and erects his normally concealed.  brightly colored crown feathers. On Puerto Rico breeding is mainly observed between February and July but nest building can begin in  November.<ref name = BPR>{{cite web |url=https://birdsofpuertorico.com/bird/220/Loggerhead-Kingbird#:~:text=Breeding%20Habits&text=Previously%20published%20reports%20indicate%20that,(Raffaele%20and%20others%201998). |title=Loggerhead Kingbird |access-date=16 June 2024 |website=Birds of Puerto Rico}}</ref> The clutch size is 2–4 eggs, except Jamaica where 4–5 eggs was the average. The adults are very aggressive towards other birds invading their territories and this may be why [[shiny cowbird]] (''molothurus bonairensis'') nest parasitism was not recorded for this species. It is a sedentary species, unlike the related [[gray kingbird]] (''T. dominicensis'') and it will move into the preferred habitat of the gray kingbird when the latter migrates away from the islands in winter.<ref name = Wiley>{{cite journal |author=James W. Wiley |author2=Orlando H. Garrido |author3=Arturo Kirkconnell |year=2010 |title=Natural History Notes on the Loggerhead Kingbird (''Tyrannus caudifasciatus'') |journal=Journal of Caribbean Ornithology |volume=23 |pages=19–30 |url=https://jco.birdscaribbean.org/index.php/jco/article/download/125/78/389}}</ref>
